ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Kerabu Mangga. Mapped to the closest parent class 'salad food product' (FOODON_00002539) as the most defensible FoodOn anchor. A more specific term for Southeast Asian dressed green mango salads is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mango, unripe — used as the primary ingredient anchor for this green mango salad preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite dish code for Kerabu Mangga. The unripe mango ingredient code A0CKM is used as the closest defensible parent anchor. Composite dish encoding would require a custom FoodEx2 facet combination not available in the current release.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for Kerabu Mangga or equivalent Southeast Asian green mango salad. SNOMED CT coverage of regional composite dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Kerabu Mangga is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small side-dish bowl accompanying a main meal of rice and grilled or fried fish.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient Composition of Malaysian Foods (IMR Malaysia MyFCD) — green mango salad composite estimate; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central raw green mango (FDC ID 169910) and HPB Singapore nutrient data for Malay salad dishes
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) / USDA FoodData Central / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for Kerabu Mangga as a composite dish. Values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD green mango and Malay salad entries, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central raw green mango (FDC ID 169910) and HPB Singapore Malay salad nutrient data. The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with these approved sources and have been retained with minor adjustments. Saturated fat estimated from kerisik (toasted coconut) and peanut fat fraction. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not available in primary sources for this composite; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Green Mango and Southeast Asian Salad Dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for raw green mango and mixed vegetable salad with dressing
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kerabu Mangga as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for raw unripe mango (GI approximately 41, low category) and mixed vegetable salads with acidic dressings (GI range 30–45). The inclusion of lime juice (acidity lowers GI), dietary fibre from green mango (2.2 g/100 g), protein from dried shrimp, and fat from peanuts and kerisik further moderates glycemic response. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (GI × available carbohydrate) / 100 = (40 × 12.8) / 100 = 5.1 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 250 mg per 100 g from fish sauce and dried shrimp
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce and dried shrimp to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling; the dish's potassium content (180 mg/100 g) partially offsets sodium load and supports vascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) driven by unripe mango, dietary fibre, and acidic lime dressing
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kerabu Mangga is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in standard serving sizes (100 g); the low GI of approximately 40 and low glycemic load of 5.1 g per serving support glycemic control, but added sugar in the dressing should be minimized and total carbohydrate intake monitored within the individual's daily plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (3.2 g/100 g), high vitamin C (45 mg/100 g), and antioxidant-rich ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kerabu Mangga is a heart-friendly side dish due to its low total fat, low saturated fat (~1.1 g/100 g), high vitamin C, and antioxidant phytochemicals from green mango and aromatics; sodium from fish sauce and dried shrimp should be controlled to avoid exceeding daily cardiovascular sodium targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C and antioxidant content from green mango and fresh aromatics
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high vitamin C content (45 mg/100 g) and antioxidant-rich profile of green mango and bunga kantan may support anti-inflammatory pathways; individuals sensitive to nightshades should reduce or omit bird's eye chili.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kerabu Mangga is a traditional Malay green mango salad originating from Malaysia and widely enjoyed across Southeast Asia, including Thailand where it is known as Yam Mamuang (ยำมะม่วง). The dish is prepared by shredding firm, unripe green mangoes into thin julienne strips and tossing them with a vibrant dressing of fresh lime juice, sugar, and fish sauce, creating a characteristic balance of sour, sweet, salty, and spicy flavors. Key aromatic ingredients include finely sliced shallots, bruised lemongrass, bird's eye chilies for heat, and bunga kantan (torch ginger flower) for its distinctive floral fragrance. Texture and umami depth are added through toasted grated coconut (kerisik), ground dried shrimp, and roasted peanuts. The salad is served raw and uncooked, making it a refreshing, minimally processed side dish. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 85 kcal, 2.5 g protein, 3.2 g fat, 12.8 g carbohydrate, 2.2 g dietary fibre, and 250 mg sodium. It is rich in vitamin C (45 mg) and vitamin A (85 mcg RAE), supporting immune function and antioxidant defense. The low glycemic index of approximately 40 makes it suitable for glycemic management. Clinically, the dish is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease in controlled portions, though sodium from fish sauce and dried shrimp warrants monitoring for hypertensive individuals. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and serves as a palate cleanser and appetite stimulant alongside grilled or fried fish and steamed rice.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for Kerabu Mangga as a named composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD green mango and Malay salad entries, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central raw green mango and HPB Singapore Malay salad data. The input AI-generated values are broadly consistent with these approved sources. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using raw unripe mango and mixed acidic vegetable salad entries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: this is a plant-based salad with dried shrimp as the only animal-derived ingredient; vitamin B12 content is negligible and effectively confirmed zero for this preparation type per USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore data. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: no vitamin D-contributing ingredients are present in this dish; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central green mango and salad ingredient data. trans_fat_g is 0.0: no hydrogenated fats are used in this traditional preparation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, and cholesterol_mg are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for this composite Malay salad dish are not available in IMR Malaysia MyFCD, USDA FoodData Central, or HPB Singapore database as of 2026-04-16. 3. Saturated fat (1.1 g/100 g) is estimated from the kerisik (toasted coconut) and peanut fat fractions based on USDA FoodData Central coconut and peanut ingredient data; this is an approximation. 4. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using raw unripe mango (GI ~41) and mixed acidic vegetable salad entries; no direct GI measurement for Kerabu Mangga exists in the database. 5. Glycemic load per serving = (40 × 12.8) / 100 = 5.1 g (low). 6. This dish is Malay cuisine; G6 multilingual rule for Singaporean dishes does not apply. Malay local name is present. Thai name (ยำมะม่วง) is included as the dish has a well-known Thai cognate. 7. FoodOn match type corrected to 'closest_parent' and FoodEx2 match type corrected to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the scoring model. FoodOn FOODON_00002539 ('salad food product') is the closest parent class; no exact composite dish term exists. FoodEx2 code A0CKM (unripe mango) is the closest parent ingredient anchor; no exact composite dish code exists for Kerabu Mangga in the current FoodEx2 release.
